name: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman
title: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (RA): The Secret Keeper
meta_description: The story of Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (RA), the companion the Prophet Muhammad trusted with the names of the hypocrites. A life of trust, sincerity, and quiet integrity.
keywords: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, story of Hudhayfah, the secret keeper, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories, trustworthiness in Islam, amanah
lede: There was one man in Madinah who walked among the people knowing a secret no one else carried. He knew, by name, every hypocrite in the city. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had told him, and him alone.

This is the story of Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (may Allah be pleased with him): the companion so far from hypocrisy that he was trusted with the names of the hypocrites themselves, and who kept that secret to the end of his life.

sections.h2: A father with a borrowed name
paras: Hudhayfah's father was a companion too, and his story is woven into his son's. He was not from Madinah, and he was not from Yemen, though everyone called him al-Yaman, the Yemeni. The name was a nickname with a story behind it.

As a young man he had been caught in a brawl and accidentally killed someone. He fled, and he kept going until he reached the city that would later be called Madinah. There he pledged his loyalty to a sub-tribe of the Ansar, a tribe traced back to Yemen, and so the people gave him the name al-Yaman.

He settled, prospered, and became a merchant with a home in Makkah and a home in Madinah, moving between the two cities easily, which made him rare. His son Hudhayfah was born in Makkah but grew up in Madinah, among the helpers of the Prophet ﷺ. The family was poor, and what little we know of them speaks of plain, unadorned lives. When the Prophet ﷺ began calling people to Islam in Makkah, they were settled in Madinah and did not hear the message from his own lips. They believed through the first man he sent ahead to teach the city, accepting that he was a Prophet of Allah without ever having seen him. Hudhayfah's whole family, his parents and his siblings, embraced the faith together.

When at last they could go to him, they went with the eagerness of people who had been waiting. Later he paired the emigrants from Makkah with the helpers of Madinah, and Hudhayfah and his father were offered a choice the others were not given: to count themselves among the emigrants or among the helpers, since they belonged to both cities. The easier path was to be an emigrant, with no one to host and no one to spend on. They chose to be helpers instead. They understood the reward in giving, and they wanted it.

sections.h2: An oath kept, even to an enemy
paras: Hudhayfah and his father missed the Battle of Badr, and the reason tells you something about both of them. They were travelling back toward Madinah, keeping their faith quiet, when Abu Jahl and his men, who were watching the roads, stopped them.

Abu Jahl questioned them. Were they going to support Muhammad? The father said it was only business: he had a place in Makkah and a place in Madinah, and travelling between them was nothing new. Abu Jahl let them go, but only after making them swear an oath that they would not fight alongside the Prophet ﷺ against him.

When they reached Madinah, they came to the Prophet ﷺ and told him about the oath, asking what they should do. They wanted to fight. His answer showed how he held to his ethics even when he was weak and outnumbered. To a man like Abu Jahl, who had hurt him in every way, he said: keep your oath, and we will seek Allah's help against them. We are not a people who betray.

By the Battle of Uhud, al-Yaman was an old man, and the Prophet ﷺ had given him leave to stay home. But he and a friend looked at each other and felt that what little life remained to them was not worth keeping back. They put on their armour and went out to fight, hoping for martyrdom rather than to die in their beds.

They reached the battlefield late, while the fighting was at its most confused. Fully covered, faces hidden by armour, they came in from the direction of Madinah just as the Muslims were being attacked from behind. In the chaos, the Muslims took the older man for an enemy.

Hudhayfah saw it happen. He saw his own father from a distance, and he saw the swords turning toward him. He screamed as loud as he could, "My father, my father," but no one could hear him through the noise of battle. By the time he reached the spot, his father had been killed by his own brothers in faith, by mistake.
The men who had done it wept and swore they had not known. And Hudhayfah, in his grief, answered with the words of the Prophet Yusuf:

ayah.text: but he said, 'You will hear no reproaches today. May God forgive you: He is the Most Merciful of the merciful.

sections.h2: Why Allah trusted him with the secret
paras: The Prophet ﷺ saw all of this: the immediate forgiveness, the refusal to hold a mistake against his brothers. He gave Hudhayfah a compensation of a hundred camels, enough to make a man wealthy for life. Hudhayfah took every one of them and gave them away to the poor among the Muslims.

He kept asking Allah to forgive those men until the day he died. This was a man of trust, of faith, of sincerity, who used no hardship for his own gain. And so the Prophet ﷺ treated him differently from all the others. He made him the keeper of a secret he gave to no one else: the names of the hypocrites of Madinah.

The weight of that secret followed him for the rest of his life. The hypocrites felt uneasy around him, afraid that one day he might expose them, but he never did. After the Prophet ﷺ passed away, Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) used to watch to see whether Hudhayfah came to pray over someone who had died. If Hudhayfah stayed away, Umar would step back too.

Hudhayfah feared hypocrisy more than most. While others asked the Prophet ﷺ about the good times ahead, the victories and the rewards, Hudhayfah asked about the trials that were coming, so he could navigate them, and about the punishment, so he would not fall into it. He used to say the cure for hypocrisy was to remember the Fire.

Hudhayfah carried more than three hundred narrations, and many of them are about the trials, the fitan, that the Prophet ﷺ described to him. The one he spoke of most was the day trust itself would leave the people. He said the Prophet ﷺ told them that trustworthiness was planted deep in the hearts of men, and then a time would come when a man would sleep and wake with it gone from his heart, leaving only a faint trace.

There would come a day, he warned, when you could hardly find a trustworthy person to do business with. People would admire a man for his intelligence, his appearance, his strength, while he had not a mustard seed of faith in his heart. He hated the loss of trust, and he was among the most trusted of all.

The same companion who memorised the warnings also carried the hope. He narrated the Prophet's words about the great pool from which he would welcome his people on the Day of Judgement, recognising them by the light of their ablution: the marks on their faces, their hands, and their feet.

sections.h2: The governor on a small donkey
paras: When the lands of the Persian empire opened, Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) sent Hudhayfah to govern its great city. The people there had been ruled by the most splendid of kings, in the largest palace of the age. They waited for their new leader to arrive in glory.

He came in on a donkey so small his legs hung down to the ground, wearing patched clothes, carrying a loaf of bread and something to eat it with. When they offered him anything he wished, he asked only for food for himself and fodder for his animal. That was all.

Later Umar called him home to test him, watching the road to see how he would return. When Hudhayfah came back in the same clothes and on the same donkey, having sat over the treasure of an empire, Umar embraced him and said, "You are my brother, and I am yours."

Hudhayfah lived just long enough to see the trials he had been warned about begin to gather, and Allah took him before they could reach him. He used to make the supplication the Prophet ﷺ taught: that if a trial was decreed that a person could not bear, Allah might take him before it came.

He died in Iraq, only days after the killing of Uthman (may Allah be pleased with him), as the discord was about to spread. When the young men around him brought an expensive shroud, he sent it back and asked for two simple white garments, saying he would keep them only a short while before Allah replaced them with something far better, or far worse, depending on his deeds.

On his last night he prayed and kept saying, "I seek refuge in Allah from waking up to the Fire." In the morning he prayed, and when death came he said, "All praise is to Allah, who saved me from the trial, who took me before it came." He wore a ring engraved with those same words: all praise is to Allah.

lessons_intro: His story is not built on a single battle won. It is built on being trusted, and on staying worthy of that trust when no one was checking.

lessons.text: He was given a secret no one else could carry, because he had shown, in grief and in power, that he would not use anything for himself.
When his own father was killed by mistake, he forgave at once, and kept praying for the men who did it until he died.

lessons.bold: Power does not have to change you.
lessons.text: He governed an empire's city and returned on the same small donkey, in the same patched clothes, asking only for his daily bread.
While others asked about the good times ahead, he asked about the trials and the Fire, so he could guard his own heart.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from among the Ansar of Madinah, known as the Secret Keeper because the Prophet ﷺ entrusted him with the names of the hypocrites. He narrated more than three hundred hadith, many about future trials.

faq.q: Why is Hudhayfah called the Secret Keeper?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ confided to him alone the names of the hypocrites in Madinah. Hudhayfah guarded that secret faithfully for the rest of his life, never exposing anyone, which is why the hypocrites felt uneasy around him.

His father, known as al-Yaman, went out to fight at the Battle of Uhud despite his age and was killed by accident by other Muslims who took him for an enemy in the confusion. Hudhayfah forgave them immediately and kept asking Allah to forgive them.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Hudhayfah?
faq.a: The weight of being trustworthy, the grace of forgiving quickly, staying humble when given power, and guarding your own heart against hypocrisy by remembering the trials and the hereafter.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Hudhayfah's life turns on a quality we rarely talk about: being safe to trust. The Prophet ﷺ handed him the one secret that could have destroyed reputations across Madinah, and Hudhayfah simply carried it, year after year, and let it die with him. He never traded on it, never hinted at it, never used it to make himself feel important.

Most of us are never asked to keep a secret that heavy. But we are asked, every day, to be the kind of person others can rely on: to return what was entrusted to us, to forgive a mistake before it festers, to stay the same whether we are watched or not. His life is an invitation to become that quiet, dependable person, and to fear our own hypocrisy more than anyone else's. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 12:92). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
There was one man in Madinah who knew a secret no one else carried.

He knew every hypocrite in the city, by name.

The Prophet ﷺ had told him, and him alone.

His father came to Madinah as a stranger, fleeing a fight he never meant to be in.

The people gave him a name that was not his own: al-Yaman.

Stopped on the road by Abu Jahl, Hudhayfah and his father were forced to swear they would not fight beside the Prophet ﷺ.

They wanted to break it for Badr.

He told them: keep your oath. We do not betray.

At Uhud, his father went out to fight, old as he was.

In the chaos, the Muslims mistook him for an enemy and killed him.

Hudhayfah forgave them on the spot: "May Allah forgive you."

The Prophet ﷺ gave him a hundred camels in compensation.

Enough to be wealthy for life.

He gave every one of them to the poor.

**Trust is earned in private.**

So the Prophet ﷺ gave him the one secret he gave no one else: the names of the hypocrites.

He kept it to his last breath.

Sent to govern an empire's great city, he arrived on a donkey so small his legs touched the ground, in patched clothes, with a loaf of bread.

He asked only for food, and fodder for his animal.

On his last night he kept saying: "I seek refuge in Allah from waking to the Fire."

His final words: "All praise is to Allah, who saved me from the trial."

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ trusted one man with the names of every hypocrite in Madinah: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (may Allah be pleased with him). He carried that secret faithfully for the rest of his life and let it die with him.

Why him? Because he had proven, again and again, that he would never use anything for himself. When his own father was killed at Uhud by mistake, he forgave the men instantly. When he was given a hundred camels, he gave them all away. When he governed an empire's city, he came and left on the same small donkey.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/hudhayfah-ibn-al-yaman

email.subject: The man trusted with every secret in Madinah
email.alt_subject: The companion who kept the names of the hypocrites
email.preview: He knew every hypocrite by name, and let the secret die with him.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There was one man in Madinah who walked among the people knowing a secret no one else carried. He knew, by name, every hypocrite in the city. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had told him, and him alone.

His name was Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (may Allah be pleased with him), and the question worth sitting with is: why him?

The answer is in how he lived. His father went out to fight at Uhud as an old man, and in the confusion of battle the Muslims took him for an enemy and killed him by mistake. Hudhayfah saw it happen and could not stop it. When the men who had done it wept and swore they had not known, he forgave them on the spot, with the words of the Prophet Yusuf: "May Allah forgive you." He kept praying for them until the day he died.

The Prophet ﷺ gave him a hundred camels in compensation, enough to make a man wealthy for life. Hudhayfah gave every one of them to the poor.

This was a man who never used hardship, or honour, for himself. So he was given the one secret that could have ruined reputations across the city, and he simply carried it.

Years later he was sent to govern the great city of a fallen empire, where the people were used to kings in splendid palaces. He arrived on a donkey so small his legs hung to the ground, wearing patched clothes, carrying a loaf of bread. He asked only for food for himself and fodder for his animal.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: trust is earned in private. We are rarely asked to keep a secret that heavy. But we are asked, every day, to be someone others can rely on, to return what is entrusted to us, to forgive a mistake before it festers, to stay the same whether or not anyone is watching.

May Allah be pleased with him, and make us worthy of trust.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: There was one man in Madinah who walked among the people knowing a secret no one else carried. He knew, by name, every hypocrite in the city. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had told him, and him alone.

His name was Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (may Allah be pleased with him). His father had come to Madinah as a stranger and been given a borrowed name, and the whole family accepted Islam before they ever set eyes on the Prophet ﷺ, believing through the first teacher he sent to their city.

His faith was tested early. Stopped on the road by Abu Jahl, he and his father were forced to swear they would not fight beside the Prophet ﷺ. They wanted to break the oath and join the Battle of Badr, but the Prophet ﷺ told them to keep it. We are not a people who betray, he said. We will seek Allah's help instead.

Then came Uhud. His father, old as he was, went out to fight, hoping for martyrdom. He reached the field late, his face hidden by armour, and in the chaos the Muslims mistook him for an enemy. Hudhayfah saw his own father from a distance and screamed his name, but no one could hear him through the noise of the swords. By the time he arrived, his father had been killed by mistake.

The men who had done it wept and swore they had not known. Hudhayfah forgave them at once, with the words of the Prophet Yusuf, and went on praying for them for the rest of his life. The Prophet ﷺ gave him a hundred camels in compensation, and he gave every one of them away to the poor.

This is why he was trusted with the secret. A man who would not use even his own grief for himself could be given the names of the hypocrites and never speak them. He feared hypocrisy in his own heart more than in anyone else's, and asked the Prophet ﷺ not about the victories to come but about the trials, so that he could pass through them safely.

When he was made governor of a great city, he arrived on a donkey so small his legs touched the ground, in patched clothes, asking only for his daily bread. He sat over the treasure of an empire and left exactly as he had come.

He died only days after the discord he had been warned about began, taken before it could reach him. His last words were a single line of gratitude: all praise is to Allah, who saved me from the trial.
storybook.pullquote: Keep your oath, and we will seek Allah's help. We are not a people who betray.
storybook.reflection: Hudhayfah was given the one secret that could have destroyed reputations across his city, and he carried it quietly to his grave. We are rarely trusted with anything so heavy, but we are asked every day to be people others can rely on: to return what is entrusted to us, to forgive quickly, and to stay the same whether or not anyone is watching.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
